flashcards.back: Indexing is a data structure technique that improves the speed of data retrieval operations by providing quick access paths to database records without scanning the entire table.
A primary index is created on a primary key column of a table. It is a dense index where each record has an index entry, and it ensures unique identification of records.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between dense and sparse index?
flashcards.back: Dense index has an index entry for every record in the data file. Sparse index has index entries for only some records (typically every block), requiring less storage but slower lookup.
A clustering index organizes records based on non-key attributes that have similar values, storing related records together in the same or adjacent data blocks.
A non-clustering index provides a separate index structure that points to the actual data records. Multiple non-clustering indexes can exist per table.

Multi-level indexing creates a hierarchy of indexes (typically 2-3 levels) where the first level is a dense index, and higher levels are sparse indexes pointing to lower levels, reducing I/O operations.
B-tree is a self-balancing tree data structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, sequential access, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time complexity.

flashcards.front: What is a B+ tree index?
flashcards.back: B+ tree is an optimized version of B-tree where all data pointers are stored only at leaf nodes, and leaf nodes are linked together for efficient range queries.

flashcards.front: Why is B+ tree preferred over B-tree for databases?
flashcards.back: B+ tree provides better performance for range queries (leaf nodes are linked), has more fan-out reducing tree height, and all data is at leaf level making searches more predictable.
Index selectivity is the ratio of unique index values to total records. High selectivity (near 1) means the index can efficiently filter records; low selectivity means many records match each value.
A composite index is created on two or more columns of a table. The index is ordered first by the first column, then by the second, etc.

flashcards.front: What is the trade-off in indexing?
flashcards.back: Indexes improve read performance but slow down write operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) because index structures must be updated. They also consume additional storage space.
A unique index ensures that all values in the indexed column are distinct. It is automatically created when a primary key or unique constraint is defined.

flashcards.front: What is the height of an index tree?
flashcards.back: The height of an index tree is the number of levels from the root to the leaf. Lower height means fewer disk I/O operations to reach data.
Index maintenance refers to keeping the index synchronized with the underlying data during insert, update, and delete operations, which adds overhead to write transactions.
